title: Extract demographic metadata from transcripts via LLM
description:
## Complexity
Medium-High (LLM-based extraction with reasoning traces, validation on 20 samples)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Context
The current cohort classification task is dominated by text signal (0.82 test F1 text-only). To properly test whether concept graphs constitute a structurally distinct modality, we need classification targets that are harder to infer from surface-level language — where text alone might achieve ~0.5-0.6, leaving headroom for graph features to demonstrate additive value.

Variable selection follows the criteria documented in `.claude/context/demographic-variables.md`: endogenous to interview content, graph-relevant, moderate text predictability, and natural uncertainty.

## Design
Extract two attributes from human-only transcript text using an LLM with structured reasoning traces:

### Attributes

1. **Career stage**: Inferred from explicit experience/timeline markers.
   - `early` — just starting, recently graduated, first job, junior role
   - `mid` — established professional, team member, regular contributor
   - `late` — senior, managing teams, 15+ years, mentoring, nearing retirement
   - `uncertain` — no clear timing evidence

2. **AI adoption stage**: Inferred from described AI usage patterns.
   - `novice` — rarely uses AI, just trying it out, skeptical
   - `experienced` — regular user, integrates AI into workflow, has opinions
   - `power_user` — custom workflows/pipelines, fine-tuning, API-level usage, teaches others
   - `uncertain` — ambiguous or contradictory evidence

### Output format
Each extraction must include a **reasoning trace**: the specific quote(s) from the transcript that support the inference. "uncertain" is the safe default — better missing data than guessed data.

### Test phase (this bead)
- Select 20 random transcripts (stratified across cohorts)
- Run DeepSeek vs Agnes on the same 20
- Compare agreement, uncertainty rates, reasoning quality
- Pick the better model for full-scale extraction

### Full extraction (follow-up bead)
- Run the selected model on all 1,250 transcripts
- Cache results as `cache/demographics.jsonl`
- Create new classification targets (career_stage, ai_adoption)
- Evaluate text-only, graph-only, and text+graph routes on these targets

## Acceptance criteria
- 20 transcripts extracted with both DeepSeek and Agnes
- Each extraction includes career_stage, ai_adoption, and reasoning trace
- Comparison table: model vs model (agreement %, uncertainty %, reasoning quality)
- Decision documented: which model for full extraction and why
- Prompt saved to `extraction/prompts/demographics_v1.txt`

## Notes
- See `.claude/context/demographic-variables.md` for the full variable selection rationale
- Prompt must explicitly instruct the model to ONLY use explicit evidence, never stereotype
- "uncertain" is the safe default — we'd rather have missing data than wrong data
- Gender was considered and rejected: not endogenous to work interviews, most transcripts lack evidence
- Education was deferred: too correlated with cohort, potentially revisit later

title: Create canonicalisation-specialist agent
description:

## Description

### Responsibilities

2. Cluster quality assessment (coherence, size distribution, outlier detection)
3. Manual review protocol (which clusters to inspect, merge/split criteria)
4. Canonical map locking — enforces immutability post-lock
5. Vocabulary drift detection (stability check, reassignment rate)

### Key files it guards

title: Evaluate canonicalisation quality and stability
description:
## Complexity
Medium (statistical analysis + drift check)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Evaluate the canonicalisation output for quality metrics and run a stability check by adding 100 held-out transcripts to measure cluster reassignment rate.

### Evaluation
1. **Vocabulary size check** — confirm each entity type falls within expected ranges (ENGINEERING.md §7)
2. **Coverage check** — what fraction of free-text labels mapped successfully? Any unmapped labels?
3. **Cluster coherence** — for each cluster, compute mean intra-cluster cosine similarity; flag clusters below 0.5
4. **Stability check** — hold out 100 random transcripts, cluster without them, then add them back and measure label reassignment rate. < 10% reassignment = stable vocabulary.
5. **Size distribution** — histogram of cluster sizes per entity type

### Tests (`tests/test_canonical_evaluation.py`)
- Coverage rate is above 95%
- Intra-cluster similarity mean is above 0.5
- Stability reassignment rate is below 10%
- Expected vocabulary ranges are met

### Acceptance criteria
- Evaluation report written (can be inline in output or separate markdown)
- All 5 checks produce results within expected ranges
- If stability > 10%: flag for review, document decision to proceed or re-cluster
- Tests pass

title: Apply canonical labels to all graphs
description:

## Recommended model

## Description

### Implementation

2. For each graph in `data/graphs/free_text/`:

### Tests (`tests/test_apply_canonical.py`)

- Non-label fields preserved unchanged
- Graph structure unchanged (same node count, edge count, edge relations)
- Validator passes on canonicalised graphs
- Handles labels not in canonical_map (logs warning, keeps original)

### Acceptance criteria

- All structural constraints still pass
- Tests pass
- Ruff clean

title: Manual review + lock canonical_map.json
description:
## Complexity
Medium (manual inspection, requires human judgment)

## Recommended model
opus

## Description
Review cluster assignments produced by clusterer.py. Inspect each cluster for semantic coherence. Merge over-split clusters and split over-merged ones. Lock `canonical_map.json` — it must never be modified after this point.

### Review protocol
1. For each entity type, print all clusters with member labels
2. Flag clusters that appear semantically incoherent
3. Flag labels that appear in the wrong cluster
4. Apply manual merges/splits
5. Write final `canonical_map.json`
6. Mark as locked (add `"locked": true` and timestamp)

### Tests (`tests/test_canonical_map.py`)
- canonical_map.json exists and is valid JSON
- All 4 entity types have entries
- No duplicate keys
- Every canonical label appears in its own values (it maps to itself)
- Locked flag is present and true
- Vocabulary sizes within expected ranges

### Acceptance criteria
- All clusters reviewed (printed for inspection)
- Merges/splits documented with rationale
- `canonical_map.json` written and marked locked
- No downstream module may modify it
- Tests pass

title: Build canonical vocabulary (clusterer.py)
description:
## Complexity
Medium (sentence-transformers + sklearn clustering)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Implement `canonicalisation/clusterer.py` to cluster free-text node labels into a canonical vocabulary.

### Implementation
1. Load all 1,250 graphs from `data/graphs/free_text/`
2. For each entity type (Construct, Value, Stance, CognitiveStyleMarker), collect all unique labels
3. Encode labels with `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` (384-dim, faster than mpnet)
4. Cluster with AgglomerativeClustering (cosine distance, average linkage, distance_threshold=0.3)
5. For each cluster, select the label closest to the cluster centroid as the canonical label
6. Output `canonical_map.json`: `{free_text_label: canonical_label}`

### Tests (`tests/test_clusterer.py`)
- Clustering produces non-empty map for each entity type
- Distance threshold 0.3 produces vocabulary in expected ranges
- Cluster centroid selection picks the label closest to the centroid
- Identical labels map to the same canonical label
- Empty input (no graphs) raises a clean error
- Handles graphs with missing entity types (e.g., no CognitiveStyleMarkers)

### Acceptance criteria
- `canonical_map.json` written with entries for all 4 entity types
- Vocabulary sizes in expected ranges (ENGINEERING.md §7): Values 15-25, Constructs 30-50, Stances 20-35, CSMs 8-12
- Cluster assignments are inspectable (print sample clusters per type)
- All tests pass
- Ruff clean

title: Phase 2 — Canonicalisation
description:
## Epic
Canonicalise the 1,250 extracted concept graphs by clustering semantically similar free-text node labels into a shared vocabulary. Lock the canonical map before any downstream modelling.

## Gate
`canonical_map.json` locked and never modified post-lock. All 1,250 graphs canonicalised.

## Scope
- Cluster free-text labels per entity type using sentence-transformer embeddings + agglomerative clustering
- Manual review of cluster assignments; merge/split as needed
- Lock canonical_map.json
- Apply canonical labels to all graphs
- Tests for clusterer and apply_canonical
- Evaluation: vocabulary sizes in expected ranges, cluster quality metrics, stability check

## Recommended agent
canonicalisation-specialist (create during phase start)

title: Scale extraction — 300 transcripts
description:
## Complexity
High (batch API orchestration + error handling + quality gates + cost tracking)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Run the winning extraction model on 300 transcripts (100 per split, stratified random sample) and cache all extracted graphs.

The extractor must:
1. Load transcript IDs stratified by split: 100 workforce, 100 creatives, 100 scientists
2. For each transcript:
   a. Check cache: if `data/graphs/free_text/{tid}.json` exists, skip
   b. Parse transcript with tagger
   c. Format for extraction
   d. Call winning model API with retry (3 attempts, backoff 2s/8s/32s)
   e. Parse JSON response
   f. Run validator; log violations but continue
   g. Write validated graph to `data/graphs/free_text/{tid}.json`
3. Log all failures to `extraction/failed.txt`
4. Print progress: completed/skipped/failed out of 300
5. Support resume: re-running picks up where it left off (cache check)

**Mid-scale quality gate (at 50 transcripts):**
- Pause after 50 transcripts are cached
- Compute aggregate validation stats: % clean, mean node/edge count, bipolarity distribution
- Spot-check 5 random graphs using the same rubric as manual review (bead 1e)
- If >20% of graphs have violations OR spot-check scores drop >1 point below 1e baseline: STOP, log findings, escalate for prompt revision
- If quality passes: log checkpoint to extraction-log.md and continue

**Cost tracking (in each graph's metadata):**
- `model_used`: model identifier
- `tokens_in` / `tokens_out`: from API response
- `estimated_cost_usd`: computed from model pricing
- Cumulative cost printed every 50 transcripts; logged to extraction-log.md

Add metadata per graph-schema.md: transcript_id, split, extraction_model, prompt_version, node_count, edge_count, bipolarity_score, speaker_turns_human, speaker_turns_ai, validation_violations.

## Prerequisites
- Winning model selected (bead 1f)
- Prompt locked (bead 1e)
- Extractor in single-transcript mode (bead 1e)
- Tagger + validator working (beads 1b, 1d)

## Acceptance criteria
1. 300 graphs cached in `data/graphs/free_text/`
2. Each graph has all metadata fields including cost tracking fields
3. All graphs pass through validator; violations in metadata
4. `extraction/failed.txt` lists failures (target: <5%)
5. Re-running completes instantly (all cached)
6. Distribution: 100 workforce, 100 creatives, 100 scientists
7. Mid-scale quality gate at 50 logged to extraction-log.md
8. Cumulative cost logged to extraction-log.md
9. `uv run pytest` still passes

## Downstream dependencies
- Blocks: Phase 2 (canonicalisation needs all graphs)

title: Model comparison experiment
Manual review — 5 transcripts
description:
## Complexity
Medium (requires API call + human judgment + iteration)

## Recommended model
opus

## Description
Extract concept graphs from 5 real transcripts using the v1 prompt, then manually review quality. This is the quality gate before model comparison.

Steps:
1. Select 5 transcripts: 2 workforce, 2 creatives, 1 scientist (mix of short and long)
2. Run extraction on each using the Anthropic API (Claude) with prompt v1
3. Run validator on each extracted graph — all must pass with zero violations
4. Manually review each graph for:
   - Are the extracted concepts genuinely present in the transcript?
   - Are the Construct bipolarities reasonable?
   - Are the relations correctly typed?
   - Are grounding spans verbatim from Human turns?
   - Are there missing concepts that should have been extracted?
5. Score each graph on the 5-criterion rubric from CHARTER.md §4 (R1–R5, score 1–3 each)
6. Record scores in `.claude/context/extraction-log.md`
7. If aggregate score < 12/15: iterate prompt (v1 → v2), re-extract all 5, re-score
8. Lock prompt version after satisfactory review

Also implement `extraction/extractor.py` in single-transcript mode:
- Load prompt from `extraction/prompts/v1.txt`
- Format transcript using `format_for_extraction()` from tagger
- Call Anthropic API with retry logic (3 attempts, backoff 2s/8s/32s)
- Parse JSON response, run validator, write to `data/graphs/free_text/{tid}.json`
- Check cache before API call
- Record `tokens_in`, `tokens_out`, `estimated_cost_usd` in graph metadata

## Prerequisites
- Dataset downloaded (bead 1a)
- Tagger working (bead 1b)
- Prompt v1 written (bead 1c)
- Validator passing (bead 1d)
- Extraction-log created (bead: 2ig)

## Acceptance criteria
1. 5 graphs extracted and cached in `data/graphs/free_text/`
2. All 5 graphs pass validation (zero violations)
3. Rubric scores recorded in extraction-log.md
4. If iterated: v2 prompt exists in `extraction/prompts/v2.txt`, v1 preserved
5. Final prompt version locked and recorded in extraction-log.md
6. `extraction/extractor.py` works in single-transcript mode with caching
7. Cost metadata (tokens_in, tokens_out, estimated_cost_usd) recorded in each graph

## Downstream dependencies
- Blocks: 1f (model comparison needs locked prompt)

title: Implement graph validator
Write extraction prompt v1
Implement speaker tagger
description:
## Complexity
Medium (text parsing + dataclasses + tests)

## Recommended model
sonnet

## Description
Implement `extraction/tagger.py` — parses raw interview transcripts into structured speaker-tagged turns.

Transcripts use `Assistant:` and `Human:` prefixes on each turn. The tagger must:
1. Split transcript text into turns based on speaker prefixes
2. Map `Assistant` → `AI`, `Human` → `Human`
3. Produce a list of `Turn` dataclass instances with `speaker`, `text`, `turn_index`
4. Provide `format_for_extraction()` that formats tagged turns using `[AI]`/`[Human]` prefixes

**CRITICAL: The regex must match `Assistant:`/`Human:` only at line start.** The word "assistant" appears frequently in interview text (e.g. "I work as an assistant", "AI assistants"). A naive split on `"Assistant:"` will produce garbage.

Also implement `tests/test_tagger.py` with coverage for:
- Normal multi-turn transcript
- Single-turn edge case
- Empty transcript → returns empty list
- Transcript with only one speaker → returns only that speaker's turns
- `"Assistant:"` appearing inside Human text → does NOT split there
- Multiple consecutive turns by the same speaker → preserves both
- Whitespace-only turn text → included with stripped text
- Round-trip: parse → format preserves content

Reference implementation in ENGINEERING.md §5.1.

## Acceptance criteria
1. `extraction/tagger.py` with `Turn` dataclass, `parse_transcript()`, `format_for_extraction()`
2. `tests/test_tagger.py` with ≥8 test cases covering all scenarios above
3. `uv run pytest tests/test_tagger.py` passes
4. Tested against one real transcript from the downloaded dataset
5. Regex uses `re.MULTILINE` and requires prefix at line start (not mid-line)
6. All imports absolute from repo root

## Downstream dependencies
- Blocks: 1e (manual review), 1f (model comparison), 1g (scale extraction)

title: Create extraction-specialist agent
Create schema-guardian agent
Phase 1 — Extraction
Fix MODULATED_BY edge-direction reversal in v4 extraction (3/1250 graphs)
description:
v4_think extraction (1250/1250 graphs, s1_data/graphs/v4_think/free_text) completed with 0.6% violation rate (7/1250 graphs, 35 total). 3 graphs (work_0821, work_0851, work_0991) account for 19 of those 35 violations via a single recurring pattern: MODULATED_BY edges with CognitiveStyleMarker as the SOURCE pointing to Construct/Stance, when the schema requires Construct|Stance->CognitiveStyleMarker (CSM as target). All other violation types are isolated/rare.

DECISION (2026-06-12): Auto-correct. Rationale:
- Only 3/1250 graphs (0.24%) affected
- Pattern is 100% deterministic: every violation is CSM→Construct|Stance for MODULATED_BY
- Rationale text confirms CSM is the modulator (e.g. "tendency shapes the construct") — semantics correct, arrow direction flipped
- Re-extraction is expensive ($) and unlikely to fix (model may repeat the error)
- The prompt v4 arrow convention (Construct→CSM = "shaped by") creates a natural semantic ambiguity: CSM causally "shapes" → LLM makes CSM the source

IMPLEMENTATION:
- Added fix_modulated_by_direction() to s2_extraction/validator.py — swaps source/target on MODULATED_BY edges where CSM is source and Construct|Stance is target
- Integrated into s3_canonicalisation/apply_canonical.py — auto-corrects during canonicalisation
- 5 new tests in test_validator.py covering: CSM→Construct fix, CSM→Stance fix, correct edges untouched, non-MODULATED_BY ignored, missing nodes handled gracefully
- Verified: all 19 violations across 3 graphs resolved (0 remaining after fix)
acceptance_criteria: All 3 affected graphs (work_0821, work_0851, work_0991) pass validate_graph() with 0 MODULATED_BY-direction violations; decision (auto-correct vs re-extract) documented; if auto-correct chosen, the correction logic is added to s3_canonicalisation pipeline (not extractor.py, to avoid re-running the cached extraction).
close_reason: Auto-correct implemented: fix_modulated_by_direction() flips CSM→Construct|Stance to Construct|Stance→CSM in validator.py, integrated into apply_canonical.py. 5 new tests. Verified all 19 violations across 3 graphs resolved.
